desc:
You've travelled over countless hills and it seems that many more hills lie
ahead of you. If only there wasn't thick fog in the valleys that seem to cause
you to lose all track of direction. Even up here on this hilltop there is some
fog and you can't really see in what direction you are truly heading. You've the
feeling that it is very easy for one to get lost in the downs, especially after
dark.
name: Northern Downs

name: Northern Downs

desc:
Out of breath from your climb up the slope of this hill, you are happy to have
finally reached the top. Up here you can almost see forever. As you slowly catch
your breath, you sadly realise there are many more hills to climb in these downs
before you reach your destination. After a bit, you begin looking for a safe way
down off this hill.
name: Wandering Path along the Ridge of a Hill

desc:
This is the perfect place for viewing the trail you must follow to reach your
destination. Up here you can see almost everything. Far off to the north you see
a line going east west that from this distance looks like trees before the road.
Both north and south there are many more ridges of hills left to traverse. These
hills peep out at you through the fog that blankets the valleys between the
hills.
name: Wandering Path along the Ridge of a Hill

desc:
The only way to traverse this hill is to weave slowly back and forth along the
incline. This is such a labour to do and you quickly begin to run out of breath.
name: A Grassy Path up a Steep Hill

desc:
The grassy downs roll out of sight in all directions as they gradually emerge
from the thick foliage in the east. Large stones and rocks naturally form a wall
to the south. With sporadic pockets of tiny trees and dense undergrowth in
certain low-lying regions, the grass is green and luxuriant.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
This forest marks where the Downs that cover the lands to the southeast come to
a stop. A large number of thorny bushes make travel through this forest almost
impossible. Indeed the plentiful supply of sunlight and rain at the edges of the
forest has made the bushes there so numerous it's only possible to reach the
outside lands if a traveller heads west through the centre and leaves the forest
there.
name: Eastern End of a Small Forest

desc:
You crawl your way through the thick bushes, gaining a few scratches on your
hands. You cant seem to find your way back, so there is just one way to go - the
random one.
name: In the Bushes

name: In the Bushes

desc:
You've travelled over countless hills and it seems that there are many more
hills to climb ahead of you. You've the feeling that this particular hilltop is
higher than the others surrounding it since the fog is less dense here. To the
north you see a small hill that seems to be set apart from the rest - a bit
taller, with a bit better view.
name: Northern Downs

desc:
This is a northward-facing bluff from which the low hills round about can be
seen. To the east is a sea of thick brownish-grey brush. To the north you can
spy the line of a road in the distance. Northwest of here is some flatter land
cut by what seems to be an old stone wall.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
The road is wide and hard-packed from many travellers over the centuries. Sets
of wheel tracks have carved deep ruts on both sides of the path, the legacy of
countless wagons and coaches that have traversed it. Brown dust covers the rocks
and pebbles found along the edge of the roadway. A wooded area can be seen to
the east.
name: Greenway

desc:
This quiet forest area stands at the base of an escarpment. One particularly
large tree dominates the landscape, a ruined old ladder clinging to its mossy
bark. Hundreds of smaller trees cluster around it, as if paying homage to their
lord. The Greenway can be seen to the west, stretching its course to Bree and
Tharbad.
name: Wooded Glade

desc:
The downs stretch away far to the south, extending in long lines of stony ridges
divided from one another by sloping valleys. Thin grass and wildflowers cover
most of the bedrock here, but several patches of bare stone push their way
through the thin layer of soil. At the bottom of the slope to the west, a narrow
brown line traces its way through a sea of green.
name: South Downs

desc:
This large road has a surface of choking dust. The hard-packed soil of the road
bears the tracks of many hooves and feet. Deep ruts have been dug into this road
by the passage of coaches and wagons over many years. The land rises to the
east, leading towards a grassy hill. The road continues its nearly straight path
to the north and south.
name: Greenway

desc:
The wide rutted road runs straight north and south here. The soil is hard and
bare after the passage of thousands of coaches and wagons. Two sets of wheel
tracks run each way on the road, obviously formed after ages of traffic. The
road is bordered by a ridge of grassy hills to the east.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway extends both north, leading to the somewhat civilised community of
Bree, and south, leading into lands far wilder. The road is wider than many, but
still barely enough for two wagons to pass one another. Short, thick grass lines
either side of the road, though that nearest it is painted in brown dust. A line
of cultivated shrubs has been planted west of the road.
name: Greenway

desc:
The wide, dusty road bends east and north here. The soil is hard and bare after
the passage of thousands of travellers. Deep wheel ruts bend in an impressive
curve through the grassy plains. A line of cultivated shrubs has been planted
along the western side of the road, but to the south they appear to have grown
wild.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The forest here is primarily composed of younger trees, with only a few older
giants towering above their lesser kin. The narrow trunks stand close to one
another, making movement difficult, but the canopy created by their branches is
not dense enough to block out the sky. The trees give way to a solid thicket of
bushes to the north and east, blocking any travel in those directions.
name: Young Forest

desc:
A gentle slope leads eastward into a valley containing rocky fields. The rich
vegetation of shrubs, bushes and grass lead upwards to a ridge of hills to the
west. An abundance of flowers can be seen growing toward the eastern end of the
downs: pale blue bellflowers, yellow pimpernels, pink knotweed, and countless
others.
name: Verdurous Backslope

desc:
This ridge is not the tallest in the South Downs, but nevertheless it towers
over the narrow roadway visible to the west. A steep bank descends toward the
road, its surface littered with loose and broken rocks. Only a few stunted trees
manage to cling to the slope. Eastward, the hill drops at a much gentler incline
that is liberally coated in grass and wildflowers.
name: South Downs

desc:
Burgeoning vegetation spreads across the plains within this valley. Flowers are
in bloom all across the terrain, thriving in a wide array of both colour and
size. A long slope begins to rise out of the valley toward the grassy downs to
the west. Rolling fields continue in every direction.
name: Blooming Fields

desc:
Thousands of small bushes dot this area, most no more than two to three feet in
height. They resemble an enormous herd of green sheep grazing on the short,
fertile grass that grows between them. The bushes become denser to the north and
west, bunching closer together until they form an impenetrable barrier.
name: Field of Bushes

desc:
Signs of erosion can be seen here as rocks lead up a steep slope to a ridgeline
to the east. This rocky outcropping is covered in grass and a few small bushes
grow amongst the stones. Massive boulders lie at the base of the slope, circled
by smaller chunks and fragments of rock. The land gradually rises to the west,
leading to an overgrown grassy field. A small house sits just to the north.
name: Base of a Rocky Escarpment

desc:
The ground here is firm and level. The valley floor itself consists of layers of
sedimentary rock that have been exposed over many years of erosion. Grass and
small bushes can be seen growing amidst the rocky terrain. Some trees can be
seen to the south, while the plains to the west grow wild with flowers.
name: Jagged Plains

desc:
A narrow valley cuts into a high scarp that overlooks it from the east. The
valley floor is itself very flat, but it is flanked by high cliffs which exhibit
layers of white and brown chalk. A dense growth of shrubs, bushes and other
plants grow at the eastern end of the valley.
name: Sheltered Valley

desc:
A dense, tangled forest begins here and stretches out toward the east, becoming
denser as it goes. Oaks, maples, and beeches dominate the area, creating a thick
but incomplete canopy overhead. A number of saplings have sprung up in the areas
where sunlight manages to reach the ground, pushing their way toward the sky. A
thick layer of dead leaves coats the land, muffling sounds.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
Saplings can be seen sprouting up in the green grass that cover this field, but
none has yet managed to grow above a few feet in height. Small, thick shrubs and
perennial flowers grow in abundance throughout this valley. Just to the east,
the dark wall of a small forest can be seen, while rolling fields extend in
other directions.
name: Budding Meadow

desc:
Green sloping downs lead eastward into a rich green valley of blooming flowers
and shrubs. Thick, wide-bladed grass fills the gaps between the other plants,
gradually becoming dominant near the base of the slope. Beyond the fields, some
saplings can be seen sprouting up in a sparse coppice. The slope rises to the
southwest until it meets a long ridge.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
This area seems to be particularly fertile, as it is covered in a wide variety
of vegetation. Several species of bushes and shrubs can be see, along with a
great deal of grass and wildflowers. Even a few plum trees have managed to take
root here. The land slopes upward toward a line of hills to the west.
name: Fertile Backslope

desc:
The grass in this valley grows in thick, dense layers, the living green shoots
barely able to push their way through the tangle of dead blades. The grass hides
holes and dips in the land, adding hazard to travelling through the area. A few
thorny bushes are scattered around the valley, becoming denser and taller as
they near a forest to the east.
name: Matted Turf

desc:
The cycle of life is well-illustrated in the generations of trees that populate
this forest. Tall, stately oak and beeches oversee dense thickets of younger
trees. Vibrant green saplings cluster around an uprooted elderly tree, whose
death leaves room for the younger generation.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
A precipitous escarpment offers a striking view of undulating, wavelike downs
that face the western horizon. This ridge, like those of the downs that flank
it, runs northeast-to-southwest, rising and falling along its way. Though the
western slope is steep and littered with rock, the top is smooth and covered in
short, soft grass.
name: Atop an Escarpment

desc:
This ridge descends eastward into a valley of grass with a small bosk. To the
west the hill angles sharply, sloping downward to a grassy field. The hills lead
off to the northwest and the southeast, a rocky divide between valleys. Years of
weather and erosion have smoothed the walls of the escarpment, leaving them
impossible to descend.
name: South Downs

desc:
These hills dip east to a backslope that leads into a grassy ravine. To the
west, a steep escarpment descends toward a wide and dusty road. The hills lead
off to the northwest and the southeast, a green and rocky divide between two
long valleys. Little grass manages to grow here, but there is an abundance of
weeds.
name: South Downs

desc:
Great stretches of grassland fill the level bottom of a valley between ridges of
downs. Tall, flaxen grass stalks grow out of a soft, reddish-brown soil,
stretching their flowering heads upward to the sky. Scattered oaks and pines
provide patches of shade; to the north, these trees grow more densely in a
forest where the blanket of tall grass fades to tufts, which there mingle with
leafy ferns.
name: Yellow Fields of Grass at a Forest's Edge

desc:
High, yellow-brown grass grows around old stumps that litter a field just south
of a forest's edge. Lichen and moss blanket the sides of the stumps, whose cores
are now hollow from decay. A jagged, grey line of rock several miles to the east
indicates a high ridge of the downs.
name: Scattered Stumps

desc:
The ground dips in a narrow trough where a gentle slope descending from the west
meets a rocky incline belonging to a high ridge that looms to the east. This
shallow trench is filled with rock and gravel which has gathered there after
falling from the eastern slope. The grass that grows here is short and pale, in
stark contrast to the taller variety that grows in the fields that stretch away
westward.
name: Trough below a Ridge

desc:
A sheer slope layered in sandstone, shale, and chalk falls away westward from
the apex of a high ridge. The exposed chalk is a brilliant white, a stark
contrast to gentler slopes both north and south where vegetation obscures the
underlying rock. The top of the ridge is covered in waves of tall grass. The
abrupt drop off to the west leads to a forest filled with aged trees.
name: Atop a Cliff Overlooking a Forest

desc:
Tall brown grass thrives on a gentle slope that stretches eastward from one of
this region's high escarpments. The long stalks bear yellow flowers and clumps
of hard, black seeds. The wispy tips of the stalks bend before the slightest
breeze; gusts can be traced through the rippling waves sent sweeping across the
grassland.
name: Rippling Waves of Grass

desc:
Clustered shrubs occupy a low valley between two ridgelines to the east and
west. Tall grass fills the spaces between shrubs, and scattered wildflowers add
touches of purple and red to the green expanse. Directly to the east, a narrow
valley cuts downward, splitting a tall escarpment.
name: Bottom of a Broad Valley

desc:
A steep escarpment cuts across a forest, separating a grove of young oaks at the
top of this slope from their more elderly kin who reign at the bottom. The
tallest trees are scarcely more than three yards in height, and their slender
trunks are only a few inches thick. Green, leafy ferns thrive in the open spaces
between the youthful oaks.
name: Tree-covered Scarp

desc:
Dozens of oaks line the edge of a steep escarpment, gazing westward over a broad
valley. These oaks are tall and broad; they appear to have aged in peace for
many years. A dark-green murk to the southwest belies a forest that fills the
valley there, while a broad meadow stretches to the east.
name: Oaks along a Ridge

desc:
Swathes of yellow, red, and purple intermingle across a fertile valley. Bees and
birds hover over the bountiful blooms, harvesting their stored nectar. These
vibrant, busy fields come to an abrupt end where a steep cliff of red and orange
sandstone topped with ghostly chalk rears up to the east.
name: Vibrant Meadow

desc:
Dense shrubs and squat trees line the gentle slopes of a fertile valley that
carves its way between two cliffs. The centre is covered in gravel and small
stones, remnants of a long-dry stream. Tufts of grass poke upward through this
rocky soil, obscuring it with splotches of green.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
This forest is filled with aged trees stooped from centuries of growth. Soft,
fuzzy moss covers the gnarled trunks, as if it were a blanket sheltering them
from the cold. Part of a large branch has broken off from one of the trees;
still attached, it leans against the tree like a cane supporting it in its old
age. Colourful mushrooms spring up between the crooked roots of the trees.
name: Elderly Forest

desc:
Tall, aged oaks rise out of a thick carpet of grass, fallen leaves, and ferns.
Thick webs of roots stretch across the ground, supporting the stately stature of
the oaks. Nests and burrows hide in earth-packed hollows beneath these roots,
which protect them from weather and the rummaging of large predators. High above
the floor, the mighty boughs of the oaks intertwine to cast the forest in
shadow.
name: Shade beneath Ancient Oaks

desc:
The wide and dusty road takes yet another bend here, going west and south
through the grassy plains. Coarse, grey-green grass lines either side of the
roadway. It has been cropped short in many spots, perhaps by horses taking a
rest here before being driven or ridden onward. A long, low escarpment lies to
the northeast, one of many found in the South Downs.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
A steep stony face leads up toward an eroding ridge that flanks this area on the
northeast. Grass covers much of the land here, replaced in spots by a few shrubs
and flowers growing between the rocks. The terrain begins to level out toward
the southwest, leading to the curving swath of a wide and dusty road.
name: Rocky Escarpment

desc:
Despite the hard, packed surface on this road, a few weeds have managed to take
hold in the stony terrain giving the road an unusual, mottled effect. Open
pastures border the eastern edge of the road while to the west thorny bushes
with wickedly sharp barbs grow along the edge of a shallow ravine.
name: Greenway

desc:
Acres of lush, fertile grassland lie all around. Small hillocks rise up from the
otherwise flat ground with the occasional shrub the only other break in the
grassy monotony. In places some of the grass has become trampled indicating
recent animal activity.
name: Grassy Pasture

desc:
An abrupt slope leads north-east toward the ridge of hills in this area. The
grassy surroundings feature a few shrubs and flowers growing between the rocks.
The terrain begins to level out to the west, leading towards a wide and dusty
road. Fields run south following along the edge of a the road.
name: Rocky Escarpment

desc:
Thick, tangled thorn bushes and woody-stemmed shrubs flourish in the rich, dark
soil that lines this shallow ravine. A few scrawny berries adorn the larger
shrubs but getting to them to pick the fruit would be tricky. The briars seem to
have overwhelmed the neat hedgerow that once lined the road to the east, turning
the decorative vegetation into an imposing obstacle. It should be possible to
find a route through the brambles to the south.
name: Briar Hedgerow

desc:
A variety of thorny bushes have engulfed this valley having seemingly grown
unrestrained for many years. Growing so closely together, the bushes have become
entwined, forming an unbroken mesh that prevents any passage to the east or
west. The very bottom of the north-south valley is lined with patches of wild
grasses.
name: Along the Briar Hedgerow

desc:
Steep banks, lined with devilishly sharp thorn bushes, surround this narrow,
secluded valley. It would take many hours and specialised tools to clear the
brambles that run rampant here. The only easy route runs from north to south
along the base of the depression where shallow puddles have collected and cannot
drain away.
name: Deep within the Briars

desc:
At the lowest point this narrow valley opens out into a marshy depression where
the heavy vegetation of the surrounding areas gives way to islands of massive
thornbushes surrounded by watery mud. Atop the steep bank to the east, masses of
briars twist and contort until they form an impenetrable barrier.
name: Muddy Briar Patch

desc:
Upon the uneven ground a great tangle of thorns has taken hold, strangling the
life out of any smaller shrub that tries to grow. Their thick, twisted limbs do
not block out all of the light, but create a dappled effect at ground level.
Small animals seem to thrive in the safe haven the thorns provide, protecting
the vulnerable creatures from circling birds. To the north the ground slopes
away into a steep-sided hollow while to the east the vegetation opens out onto
the Greenway.
name: Head of a Thorny Valley

desc:
The majority of the field is covered with a rich blanket of grass interspersed
with small shrubs that have been chewed by passing wildlife. Tracks of various
animals have been left in the soft soil and several of these tracks disappear
though a narrow gap in the hedge to the west.
name: Field bordering the Greenway

desc:
The Greenway lives up to its name here as it runs straight and true between two
thick, green hedges. To the west a dense tangle of bushes run along the edge of
a steeply sloping ditch while the smaller shrubs bordering the road to the east
separate the highway from the flat, grassy fields beyond.
name: Greenway

desc:
To the east, a narrow trail climbs up the side of the ridge, snaking back and
forth as it seeks out the easiest route to the top. Underfoot, small stones
stick out among the thin grass, providing a habitat for all manner of buzzing
insects. To the west grass reigns, spreading like a carpet over the earth.
name: Bottom of a Goat Trail

desc:
In the shadow of hills that loom high above in the north and east, a solitary
tree grows tall and proud. Its presence here is an anomaly, but presumably the
result of a single seed having been deposited by a bird and surviving against
the odds. Surrounding the tree a sea of tall grass carpets the land, fading away
to barren rock as the ground rises up to the escarpment to the east.
name: Lone Tree in the Grassland

desc:
This stretch of the Greenway runs directly from the north to the south. The road
surface is wide, but dense, thorny bushes growing on the western edge have
encroached over the road, forcing passing wagons to slow as they negotiate the
obstacle. A carefully built dyke lines the eastern edge dividing the busy road
from the farmland beyond.
name: Greenway

desc:
The road here is pitted with holes, ruts, and cracks in the hard ground, most
likely damage caused by wagon wheels. A narrow opening in the thorn bushes to
the west seems to lead to a narrow valley while the face of a hillside rises
almost vertically to the east. Despite the sheer, rocky surface, the hill is
covered in greenery.
name: Greenway

desc:
The hard, bare soil of the road bears the marks of many passing wheels. Deep
ruts along the middle of the road are parallelled by more ruts, perhaps where
wagon drivers have tried to avoid the worst parts of the road. Weeds and small
shrubs grow alongside the road, but are kept at bay from the road's surface.
name: Greenway

desc:
A gentle slope descends from a high ridge to the west. The vegetation that
blankets this slope is lush and fertile. Clumps of cowslips peek above thick
grasses, dotting the slope with brilliant patches of yellow. The soil is soft,
and its colour is a rich and fertile black.
name: Verdant Slope

desc:
The lowest point of a valley stretches north to south in the shadow of a steep
escarpment to the east. The terrain swells gently to another ridge several miles
to the west. Clusters of wildflowers thrive here: sunny cowslips, violet
rampion, and red-and-white orchids.
name: Field of Wildflowers

desc:
Great, rolling plains lie before you, the ground rising and falling as it passes
between the two prominent hills to the northeast and southwest. In the east, the
grassland fades away to become rough, stony plains. Tulips and other bulbous
flowers grow here between rabbit holes, and faint tracks, perhaps made by foxes,
circle the holes.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Vegetation flourishes here. Yellow buttercups cluster together at the base of
the dyke that runs along the western edge of the field, as if huddling out of
the wind, while daisies spread themselves out on the thick carpet of lush grass.
The southern edge of the field slopes steeply, rising to form one of the many
hills in the South Downs.
name: Flower-filled Plain

desc:
A vast field covered by grass and wildflowers reaches away in all directions,
although to the west the horizon is dominated by a rocky ridge. Quite a few
pebbles and some larger stones litter the field and among them grow prickly
shrubs and small thorn bushes. A few wisps of fur caught on the thorns give
proof of wild animals passing through here from time to time.
name: Expanse of Grassland

desc:
From the top of the downs you can see the land rolling out to the north and east
over rich, fertile grassland until it reaches a rocky ridgeline on the horizon.
To the south the downs rise and fall in austere splendour, devoid of flora,
bleak and forbidding. To the west the Greenway cuts through the land, leaving a
wide, straight scar on the landscape.
name: South Downs

desc:
Rising high above the surrounding plains, this hill offers fresh, clean air and
a commanding view of the surrounding landscape. Underfoot the ground is stony
with little vegetation clinging to the peak. To the east the hills flatten
totally and turn into a wide expanse of grass, while the Greenway and its
passing traffic flanks the hill to the west.
name: South Downs

desc:
Grass grows thick, providing a plentiful supply of food for any grazing animals
that pass through. Wildflowers cover these uneven fields and provide a steady
supply of nectar for bees. A short chain of hills rises gently to the west; in
every other direction the grass stretches away as far as the eye can see.
name: Exposed Meadow

desc:
A few planks have been placed across the branches and tied down to form a
platform large enough to support a few children. A number of worn etchings can
be seen; it appears that someone carved initials into the wood some years ago.
Scraps of cloth and pieces of wood lie abandoned on the platform, and a piece of
metal glitters where it has gotten stuck beneath two planks. Thick branches
surround the platform, preventing you from seeing much of the landscape beyond.
name: Abandoned Treehouse

desc:
Instead of the familiar green colours that are found elsewhere in this region,
this field is unusual because it has been graced with patches of colourful
blossoms. At some point in the past a fire must have burned here, clearing away
large areas of grass and allowing wildflower seeds to prosper.
name: Herbaceous Plains

desc:
A steep slope rises to the east, its face composed of many-coloured bands and
layers of sandstone and almost bare of vegetation. The soil at the base of the
hillside is mostly sand and supports only a few scraggly weeds. Further east,
the land becomes richer and is covered in a dense layer of low grass surmounted
by tall spires of yellow and orange wildflowers.
name: South Downs

desc:
The line of hills curves from north to east here, dipping only slightly from a
tall hilltop to the north. A broad valley stretches out to the west, though it
would take a lengthy climb to reach it from here. Cracks and fissures riddle the
ridgeline, some so small that they are barely visible, others of sufficient size
to swallow an unwary hobbit, or even a man.
name: High atop the Downs

desc:
A tall, rocky slope rises very steeply to the east, blocking off any chance of
travel in that direction and keeping this area in deep shadow throughout the
morning hours. Chunks of sandstone have broken off from the escarpment and now
line its base. Further to the west and south, weeds and ground creepers cover
the land, gradually giving way to short grass.
name: Below an Escarpment

desc:
Patchworks of colours and textures fill this area. Some of the grass has grown
long and some has been cropped short where grazing animals have had their fill.
Bluebonnets, buttercups and cowslips provide vibrant bursts of colour amid the
sea of grey-green grass. The wildflowers have taken hold and prospered in the
arable land.
name: Wildflower Pasture

desc:
Tall, woody brambles and rocks, fallen from the ridge above, are spread across
these plains, making it hard to follow the animal tracks that wind their way
across the land. Only one well-travelled route stands out as it picks its way up
the hill towards the peak high above. Seemingly endless grassland can be seen
westwards from here.
name: Plains before a Ridge

desc:
This cave looks like it started as a crack in the rock that has gradually grown
wider over time as water has washed out the stone. The cavern is no more than
ten feet across in most spots, but it runs back into the hillside for a hundred
feet or more. A thick layer of coarse, dry sand covers the floor of the cave.
name: Narrow Cavern

desc:
This small house looks like it is barely large enough for one inhabitant, and as
only one bed can be seen, that is likely the case. A tiny table sits against the
east wall, a crude three-legged stool in front of it. A shelf is set up by the
door, bearing a few meagre supplies. A tattered blanket lies at the base of the
bed and smells strongly of dog.
name: Small Cottage

desc:
The ridge empties down into the valley floor, the terrain evening out across the
downs. A rich vegetation of shrubs, bushes and grass dominates the gently
sloping landscape. Several tiny ravines cut their way through the vegetation,
exposing the layered sandstone that forms the bedrock beneath the soil.
name: Verdant Slope

desc:
This rocky precipice gives travellers a unique vantage point from which to look
down on the grassland that spreads out below. To the south the ridge continues
but is badly eroded and care must be taken to avoid slipping on the loose scree.
There are some scattered weeds fighting for a meagre existence amid the rubble,
but very little other fauna survives in this rocky terrain.
name: Prominent Peak

desc:
The land falls away in a sweeping slope toward the northeast, while a ridge
rises to the southeast. Exposed humps and dips of sandstone worn away at uneven
rates dominate the terrain, interspersed with patches of dense grass and bushes
clinging to the sandy soil. The slope becomes less pronounced to the east.
name: Rock-strewn Slope

desc:
A sweeping hillside descends gradually from the west to form this broad valley.
Grass and rocks are evident in equal parts, vying with one another in a display
of green and ochre. Scattered bushes nestle in low-lying areas and appear to be
hunched down to the ground, perhaps to avoid the strong winds that often stream
down the slope.
name: Sloping Valley

desc:
This valley is nestled against the base of an escarpment. Stones broken off from
the hillside litter the ground, extending quite some distance from the base of
the cliff. Tall grass flourishes amongst the rocks, interspersed by a wide
variety of colourful wildflowers. Cultivated land and a couple of large
buildings are visible to the south.
name: Rocky Valley

desc:
This farm is quite sizeable, covering well over a hundred acres, and must be
tended by several people. The land is planted in wheat and oats, which seem to
thrive in the rich soil. The fields are bordered on the east by a barn and to
the south by a large house. A small area is fenced off next to the barn, no
doubt intended to hold animals of some sort, but it is currently empty.
name: Large Farm

desc:
This slope descends from west to east, becoming flatter as it goes. The ground
is uneven, pierced in many places by upthrusts of bare stone in a wide range of
brown, orange, and dusky yellow hues. Dense clumps of short grass cling to the
ground, interspersed with tall, narrow blades of a different variety. A large
farm is visible just to the east.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
The land slopes steeply up to the west as it approaches a crest, while it
descends more gradually to the east. The terrain is rocky and uneven, with deep
grooves cut into the surface by ages of erosion. Low grass and heather cling to
the hillside, overshadowed in a number of places by small clusters of bushes.
name: Steep Slope

desc:
This hilltop seems to be the highest point in the downs for miles around. The
western face drops away precipitously to a broad valley far below. The eastern
side of the hill is also quite steep at first, but becomes much shallower after
a quarter mile or so. A few stunted bushes cling to the hilltop, fighting to
maintain their grip against the winds that sometimes scour this landscape.
name: Atop the Downs

desc:
A rocky ridge rears upward from the valleys to either side, its jagged line
stretching north and south. Its western face hangs slightly over a trough some
distance below, as if it were a wave about to crash. Grey boulders coated in
moss rest atop the ridge, surrounded by small shrubs and hardy wooden plants.
Other high ridges can be spotted far to the east and west, a continuation of the
seemingly endless waves of downs.
name: Crest of a Ridge

desc:
The South Downs stretch out in every direction in long ridges and wide valleys.
This ridge continues both to the northwest and southeast for many miles. Thin
grass has taken hold along the hilltop, interspersed with thistles and other
weeds. A few short pine trees have driven their roots into cracks in the rock
and stand as silent sentinels over the land.
name: Steep Scarp

desc:
The land levels out into a broad, nearly flat field. Low, thick grass covers
most of the area, but it is highlighted by dozens of varieties of flowers in an
array of different colours. A tall, bare scarp rises to the north and east. The
valley stretches further to the south, while west of here cultivated fields
surround a large farmstead.
name: Beneath a Steep Scarp

desc:
A tall, barren hillside rises to the east until it reaches a stony ridgeline.
Only a few stunted shrubs cling to cracks in the hillside, which is otherwise
clear of any vegetation. Patches of loose rock threaten to come tumbling down at
any minute, and a thick layer of scree at the base attests to this happening
many times before.
name: Base of a Steep Scarp

desc:
As the plains extend eastward, they drop further into a deep valley. Here the
land rolls in a series of dips and hillocks like rolling waves upon the sea.
Emerald grass covers much of the land, broken in spots by stretches of exposed
rock. Dozens of isolated trees stand like islands among the grass, mostly oaks
and maples. A large farmstead is just north of here.
name: Rolling Lowlands

desc:
The ridge runs directly north from here, but bends somewhat to the east as it
extends to the south. The ridge is not level, but instead rises and falls in
uneven crests and dips, making travel along it more difficult. Only a small
amount of grass and weeds are able to find purchase on the otherwise bare stone.
name: Uneven Ridgeline

desc:
This exposed ridgeline is broad and flat, easy a hundred feet across. It
stretches like a bridge between the rocky cliff in the north and a broad, grassy
crest to the east. A faint line, no wider than a goat trail, marks an easy route
leading down from the hills onto the grassy plateau below. Little vegetation
grows on the rocky surface of the ridge other than a few tufts of yellowing
grass.
name: Bend in the Ridge

desc:
This crest in the downs stretches from northwest to southeast, bending until it
points almost due south here. The western face is steep and barren of nearly all
vegetation. The eastern slope, though still steep, is far gentler, and has
accumulated a thick layer of soil that now supports dense grass.
name: Broad Ridge

desc:
Nestled between two long bands of downs, this slope descends toward a deep
valley to the east. The stone in this area has eroded away at varying rates,
creating a maze-like formation of walls and crevices. Lanky weeds and pale grass
struggle to maintain a grip on the scant soil that remains.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
This valley lies nestled between two ridges in the South Downs. The terrain
rises to both the east and west, and a thick layer of soil has accumulated here,
providing abundant nutrients to a wide variety of grasses, weeds, and
wildflowers. A number of small shrubs are also scattered around. A thick pile of
broken stone rests at the base of an escarpment to the east.
name: Deep Valley

desc:
Rugged slopes to the south and west stretch away from a ridge that curves from
north to east. At the highest point of the ridge, occurring at the middle of the
bend, there is a high, rugged outcrop. A nest made of branches, twigs and leaves
hides sheltered in a crevice near the top of the outcrop.
name: Bend on a Ridge

desc:
A great earthen wave rises to an apex, its western slope a steep descent to the
trough, its eastern a gentle descent to a rocky hollow. This ridge is rugged and
crowned with boulders. Short, dry grass and drooping flowers struggle to grow in
the thin soil between the rocks.
name: Crest of a Ridge

desc:
The walls of this expansive inn are made of wood and decorated with hunting
trophies of long-forgotten creatures. Three long tables and plenty of wooden
chairs cover the floor. A huge lantern hanging from the ceiling throws a
flickering light all around the room.
name: Forsaken Inn

desc:
Only a few walls and broken stones remain of what used to be a building. These
walls seem to be the ruins of a chapel. Many broken blocks which once have
formed the building lie scattered around and make travelling difficult. Most of
the stones, as well as the complete floor of the chapel, have been overgrown by
grass, bushes, and vines.
name: Old Ruins

desc:
The centre of this large road is so hard that only vast amounts of rain could
turn it muddy. The edges, however, have not been packed as hard and seem to turn
into soft mud quite often. The surrounding grasses try to take root on the road
wherever it does not get constantly destroyed by hoofs of horses, feet of
travellers or iron wheels of carriages.
name: Greenway

desc:
Cutting through the grassland, the Greenway connects the north with the south.
The soil is darkened by dirt and firm from many travellers' feet. A few high
bushes on the western side of the road offer a change of scenery for the eyes
while on the eastern side an old and overgrown camp-site can be spotted.
name: Greenway

desc:
Many piles of boulders and rocks lie scattered around in the high grass. Most of
them are, at least in parts, overgrown by grass or low bushes. The road of firm
soil leads through this assembly of rocks and bends around the occasional larger
pile or boulder.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway bends sharply to the east and south here, with the surrounding
overgrown grasses encroaching onto the roadway. Columns of smoke, probably from
the many chimneys of Bree, can be seen to the north. The hard soil of the roads
bear many tracks of carts and feet.
name: Greenway

desc:
The surrounding field is cut through by a bending curve in the wide,
oft-travelled Greenway. The road curves along the southern base of a nearby down
and continues south along a light forest and west through the fields that dot
the landscape south of Bree-hill.
name: Greenway

desc:
This small forest covers only a few square miles of land, lying between the
plains in the north, the Greenway to the west and south, and the Downs to the
east. Bushes with sharp thorns grow all over the forest, but they seem to be
most thickly-clustered near its edges. And yet, to the west, there is a small
opening in the mass of bushes that would allow passage out of the forest.
name: Small Forest

desc:
Row by row, the vines grow. Every single plant seems to have been planted in a
planned spot. The complete vineyard looks to have been in order at one point in
time. The soil beneath the plants is full of debris, dead leaves, and various
other dead vegetation.
name: Overgrown Vineyard

desc:
Literally thousands of vines can be seen here, overgrown and snaking all about
with no real direction. Small bands have been used to bind each vine to a thin
stake to keep its growth upright. As far as the eye can see, each row stands in
a nearly perfect order, yet obviously neglected over time.
name: Abandoned Vineyard

desc:
Vines are growing here, set up in one line after another. Stakes, firmly planted
into the ground, hold the vines upright while narrow walls of quarried stones
divide the whole area into several parcels. Nature seems to have long ago
claimed this place.
name: Ruined Vineyard

desc:
A vast sea of lush, green grass dominates the landscape, tall and bending in the
wind with each gust. Low bushes and small trees grow intermittently throughout
the area, providing more than adequate shelter for the variety of small animals
who have dug burrows throughout the land.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A grassy hill ascends suddenly from the otherwise flat landscape surrounding it.
Mounds of dirt and small holes are scattered about, evidence that small animals
are plentiful on the hill. The hill provides an excellent view of the landscape
for miles all around.
name: Grassy Hill

desc:
This low, flat area stretches wide between several areas of rolling hills in
every direction. Sparse patches of trees and bushes are scattered throughout the
flat landscape and the grass is very tall and overgrown. There are several
mounds and holes spread throughout the area, where animals have burrowed.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Lush green grass spreads out as far as the eye can see. The meadow is dotted
with small, yellow flowers, which grow plentiful all around. A low rock wall,
ruined with the passage of time, blocks travel to the north. The wall appears
unstable, built from large grey rocks that have large gaps between them.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
This is the northern end of a wide area of smooth slopes and hills which
traverse up and down. Only low vegetation can be seen, the highest bushes
standing no more than three feet tall. Hard grasses grow on the hills to the
south, and the Greenway cutting the grasslands from north to south is clearly
visible.
name: North End of the Barrow-downs

desc:
A faint trail meanders through tall grasses, parts of it overgrown with tufted
weeds whose slender stems move with the breath of the wind. The surrounding
plain is not cultivated though faint, uniform ruts in the ground indicate that
it might once have been.
name: Overgrown Field

desc:
An old cobbled path twists and turns through old fields, with broken stone walls
lining either side. The sides of the walls are collapsed, with large stones
strewn about, causing the cobbled path to become treacherous. Causes of the
destruction could be from the numerous fallen trees that lie over the path.
name: Broken Path

desc:
The trees of the Chetwood forest seem younger compared to north of here where
they grow thick and tall. Beyond the faded trail underfoot, the terrain changes
into rolling plains of grassland to the south, interrupted only by a few shrubs.
An occasional call of a hunting wolf or the chirps of birds breaks the silence
of this field and the lands nearby.
name: Forest Border

desc:
There is little to be seen here other than the plains and grasslands that
stretch into the distance. An ill-defined trail extends from east to west,
though it's not always easy to make out its path. To the north, the trees of the
Chetwood stand together in silent ranks, reaching to the sky. Downs to the south
prevent travel that way.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
The trail coming from the west ends abruptly here. To the east, massive piles of
rock and hard earth slope inward from the cliffs on either side. On the northern
end, loops and knots made of woven plant fiber peek out from the debris and
splintered slats of wood lay scattered across the rubble.
name: Abrupt End of a Trail

desc:
Grass and small bushes grow in abundance here close to the village of Bree. The
tall trees of the Chetwood forest can be seen far to the north, beyond the
rundown path that stretches east and west. A much smaller forest lies to the
south, with many kinds of trees mixed together and large, thorny bushes
inhibiting any travel into it from this direction.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This grassy area is the top of a large down, and a steep escarpment makes travel
to the south impossible. The northern side of the down slopes toward a
well-travelled trail, beyond which a wide field spreads along the base of
Bree-hill. A bend in the Greenway can be seen curving away from the southern
base of this down, bordered on one side by light forest.
name: Grassy Down

desc:
The remains of an old barn lie strewn about. The walls have long fallen down,
and heavy timber frames now lie on the ground covered in moss. An old well lies
broken in the centre with the stone walls having caved in. Patches of flowers
are all that remain to give life to the abandoned farm.
name: Abandoned Farm

desc:
The landscape is dotted with flowery grasses and small, prickly plants that
continue north toward Chetwood Forest. On a clear day, the wood can be seen on
the far horizon. The ground is covered with mounds and holes made by small,
burrowing animals who have taken the land as their home. The path to the east is
blocked by a precipitous and eroding rock.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
The grasses and shrubbery that inhabit these fields stretch out to the east
until they are abruptly stopped by a cliff in the distance. Rocks jut out here
and there, dotting the landscape with their massive forms. A small impenetrable
forest lies to the south.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
Several towering ash trees are lightly distributed across the landscape. To the
east, the ground rises quickly, becoming a sea of rolling hills lapping at the
sky in the far distance. To the west, dense clusters of shrubs and bushes crowd
the edges of a small but thick wood.
name: Light Forest below the Downs

desc:
Grassy downs roll up and down in all directions, some creating steep cliffs,
others flattening out smoothly into valleys and plains. Grass and small shrubs
grow in abundance amid the rocks that dot the landscape. A small, rock-strewn
path winds its way precariously upwards toward a higher plateau on the downs,
possibly offering a better vantage point of the surroundings.
name: Steep Hillside

desc:
The terrain here is rocky with some smaller patches of grass mixed in. Passage
west is stopped by a sheer cliff that drops down toward a field, its edge
crumbling like the corners of a stale biscuit. A valley is formed by a downward
slope to the east where a small glint of flowing water can be seen.
name: Downs Overlooking a Valley

desc:
A narrow valley tucked among the rockier topography of the neighbouring downs to
the west and east is filled with a variety of plant life. The field is littered
with debris, mostly broken cobblestones of various sizes. A little spring
supplies fresh water to thirsty animals and travellers.
name: Narrow Valley in the Downs

desc:
Rolling hills rise above the Chetwood forest to the north, and a steep but not
tall slope leads to that forest. To the west, a small and poorly maintained
trail ends in an abrupt fall over a cliffside to the fields below, prohibiting
travel in that direction.
name: Downs Overlooking a Forest

desc:
The trail continues through the forest to the north. Eventually, it gives way to
rocks standing amidst the grass and shrubs of the Downs as the land rises toward
here. Beyond the path, a road can be seen making its way through the forest,
most likely the Old East Road, leading to Bree in the west and toward Weathertop
far to the east.
name: Elbow in a Faded Trail

desc:
The hillside falls away steeply from this small down in most directions, making
travel impossible except back to the east. The ground is covered with rocks and
tough shrubs that have found a place to take root in the soft soil.
name: Small Plateau

desc:
The fields start to slope slightly upwards as they come closer to the Downs in
the west. The terrain there is rockier and the grasses and shrubs grow more
sparsely. An occasional winged insect buzzes by on its way to fulfil some
unknown mission.
name: Sloping Fields

desc:
The grasslands are flat and dotted with small and medium-sized boulders of
various forms. The grass is lush and green, with bits of bush sprinkled about.
The path is blocked by a short but steep plunge into the plains below. The top
of the Down reaches north and south.
name: Top of a Down

desc:
This wide area is beneath the cliff to the east and lies above several Downs
lower to the west. However, the sharp rocks along the edges, which are such a
contrast to this mainly grass-covered spot, prevent further travel to the lands
below. There seems to be no way to move but back up to the hilltop above. Small
rocks lie here ready to be kicked over the side.
name: Grassy Peak of a Down

desc:
This is the highest point of the nearby Downs and the land to the west spreads
out below. The western edge is crumbling somewhat except along a small section
which allows travel downwards to another level of the downs. To the north lies a
crumbling, rocky slope that prevents travel toward the forest. A large patch of
thorny shrubs prevents travel eastwards.
name: Top of the Downs

desc:
The fields narrow down to a pathway no more than a dozen feet wide with towering
boulders blocking the view to the east and thorny shrubs blocking any travel to
the west. To the north, small bushes dot the landscape of grassy plains.
name: Path between Boulders

desc:
Rich vegetation composed of myriad bushes, shrubs, and grasses grows here,
ending abruptly to the east. There, the chalk hillside rises steeply and makes
passage impossible. To the west, the Downs rise slowly as the uphill terrain
gets more rugged. Southwards the land eases out into wide fields.
name: Fields below a Steep Hillside

desc:
Towering hillsides, rocky and treacherous, block travel to the east and north.
Taller downs peek above their harsh slopes, rising toward the heavens above. To
the west, the ground flows downhill on a slope, opening up into a valley below.
name: In the Downs

desc:
The earth drops away to the east into a grassy valley among the Downs, while a
precipitous drop to the west makes passage nearly impossible. There is an
infrequently used track that leads down and cuts back a few times to make the
descent easier.
name: High in the Downs

desc:
A long, narrow room has been built into the chalk of the cliff. It is not more
than eight feet at the widest, but stretches back more than thirty. The walls
are smooth and unblemished. There is a messy mixture of boxes, sacks, cupboards
and other furniture around the room. A sturdy door is located on the western
wall.
name: Bandit's Hideout

desc:
Sparse woody vines cover parts of the nearby lush green fields. The vines grow
most densely around what was once a building but is now fully decayed beneath
the vines, providing an ideal nesting spot for the local fauna. Smaller parts of
the vines appear to be constantly moving about, looking for a new location to
grow.
name: Vine-covered Field

desc:
Rocky hills rise from the otherwise fertile surroundings. The eastern slope of
the largest hill seems to have collapsed in a landslide, creating a rocky and
impassable wall at the base of it. Small patches of green grass sprout up where
it can find space between rocks.
name: Eroded Hillside

desc:
Boulders of all sorts and sizes dot the landscape. Some lie together in large
piles, piling on top of each other, others seem thrown randomly around the area
without any discernible order. Yellowish grass grows smoothly between the rocks,
the colour changing to a dull brown around each rock.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
Low shrubs and brush are sparse but plentiful. Dead and dying vegetation lie low
to the ground beneath it. Low hills can be seen on the horizon in most
directions. To the south, a large patch of rocks and boulders seem to outline
the entrance to a cave in the hillside.
name: Sparse Brush

desc:
The grassy downs rise gradually from the dense brush in the north and continue
to roll out of view to the south and west. The grass is green and lush, with
scattered patches of small trees and thick brush in some low-lying areas. Animal
paths have been blazed into the ground where passage is the least cumbersome.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
Low hills rise above dense brush in the north and west. The hills are carpeted
with a dense, lush, green grass. Earthen paths are blazed into the ground where
animals and other passengers have travelled. Small, scattered patches of rocks
lie here and there throughout the hills.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
The brush is thin and mostly bare here, but it grows thicker to the north. The
land stretches between the dense brush and gradually rises to the west into
rolling, grassy downs. Animal tracks can be spotted intermittently among the
bare, earthen patches in the ground.
name: Flat Field

desc:
Countless animals have beaten a path through the brushwood. In places it becomes
so narrow and low that the branches of the bushes on both sides have met and
ensnared each other. Many of the vegetation's lower leaves have been nibbled off
by grazing animals.
name: Brushwood

desc:
A lush green carpet of dense grass covers the hills. Low hills roll in all
directions, mostly grassy but with a few rocky patches scattered about. A small
patch of dense brush and small trees grow in a low area, offering a suitable
shelter for whatever wildlife might inhabit the area.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
The surrounding plains rise gradually into some rolling downs. The lush green
grass carpets the hills in a thick, soft blanket. The hills provide a vantage
point of the surrounding plains and brush thickets. Wherever it is easiest to
travel, animal pathways have been driven into the ground.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
This small hill rises slightly lower than the surrounding hills, which obscures
the view to the south and west. The hill is smaller in nature and the sides are
smooth and neat. Small grey pebbles lie strewn across the knee-high grass, some
of which slowly roll down the hill.
name: Small Hill

desc:
Bare earthen animal paths wind between and over the hills, blazed into the lush
green grass that blankets the hills. The hills are taller here, providing a
great vantage of the surrounding area. In the east, the large Bree-hill
dominates the horizon.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
The ground of the low hills is mostly comprised of small, broken rocks and
gravel. Patches of bright green grass pop up throughout the field of rock. To
the north, the ground suddenly drops over a steep, rocky descent. Rolling,
grassy downs can be spotted on the western horizon.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
Old, broken furniture has been discarded in the cellar, piled in corners with
cobwebs floating everywhere. The uneven ground is slippery, with puddles pooling
in the deep sections. Pale, white mushrooms grow in the corners, their spores
wafting in the air. A large, faded, and torn tapestry covers the eastern wall,
obviously covering a large hole in the wall.
name: Forgotten Cellar

desc:
A cramped tunnel, approximately eight feet in height and three feet wide, winds
its way underground. It branches off to the north and to the south. The walls
and floors have been worn smooth, evidence that the tunnel has been heavily
travelled at some point in time.
name: Cramped Underground Tunnel

desc:
This is a large underground area with plenty of room in which to manoeuvre.
Several chairs lie haphazardly around the centre of the cavern, with several low
tables lining the walls. The tables are covered in parchments and books. A large
bookshelf stands against the northern wall, and maps line the walls all around.
name: Bandit Hideout

desc:
The tunnel ends abruptly here, for no discernible reason. The walls, ceiling,
and floor are all formed from smooth, grey stone. Cool, fresh air wafts in from
a large crack in the ceiling above, and there are large cracks in the southern
and eastern cavern walls.
name: Dead-end Tunnel

desc:
Rocks and gravel blanket the floor of a wide, tall cavern. Piles of dry grasses
lie strewn about, some hidden behind large rock formations or boulders. The
ceiling is formed from bare earth, and small, loose parts occasionally fall.
name: Bear Den

desc:
The hills here are surrounded by natural rock walls in all directions, formed
from rock formations rising from the earth. Passage to the north seems
impossible due to some loose rocks and boulders which have formed a large
fissure, but appear to fill it. A large area of dense brush looms in the south.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
This long ridge of the South Downs runs south to north here as it borders the
Greenway to the west. The eastern slopes are rocky and steep, making travel that
way impossible. To the south and north the ridge is narrow, but fairly easily
traversable.
name: Hills along the Greenway

desc:
Surrounded by thick, thorny hedges, this broad and dusty road is more or less
overgrown by tough grasses and ferns. The tracks themselves are hard and bare
after the passage of merchants' carriages. The road continues to the north and
south, a long straight line going on for miles and miles.
name: Greenway

desc:
The hills of the South Downs give way here to a low valley of wavy grass. To the
north, the grass expands into a wide green field. While in all other directions,
the hills rise in an imposing vista of rocky mounds. A few lines of dirt,
interrupted by the occasional brown circle, indicate the presence of some
subterranean mammals.
name: Valley among the Hills

desc:
The rocky face of the rocky promontory rises steeply from the surrounding
landscape. Its jagged peaks and craggy edges seem to scrape the sky. The huge
plateau at the top of the promontory stretches out before them, a barren expanse
of bleak rock that glints in the sun.
name: Rocky Promontory

desc:
The hillocks swell like waves of earth and stone within the verdant expanse of
the South Downs. Bushes, sparse yet tenacious, sprout from the soil at the
junction, their forms twisted by the rugged landscape. Among them, the low forms
of bearberry and crowberry trace the contours of the hillocks, their leaves a
vibrant green against the earthy tones of the terrain.
name: Junction of the Hillocks

desc:
Upon this rocky ridge, large oaks find their dominion. They grow at various
angles, their forms twisted and gnarled. Their roots burrow deep into the cracks
and crevices of the stone. The branches of the oaks sway slowly, their movements
a silent dance with the unseen wind. The ground is a patchwork of stone and
soil, the former peeking through the latter like bones through the skin.
name: Rocky Hilltop

desc:
Hilltops rise from the land, their forms cloaked in a verdant sea of brush that
softens the harshness of the stone. Every exposed surface is hidden beneath this
living tapestry. Amidst the verdant sea, patches of mosses and lichens cling to
the exposed stone, their forms tracing the contours of the land. The hilltops
are a treacherous passage, the undulating terrain and dense vegetation creating
a challenge for the unwary traveller.
name: Brush-covered Hilltops

desc:
This area of broken rocks and boulders is a short way downslope from a tall
ridgeline to the west. Little vegetation has managed to take hold here, leaving
the land mostly barren. A thick layer of sand and shattered rock fragments coats
the ground, leaving only enough nutrients for the hardiest of weeds. A steep
slope leads downward toward the east, but a deep crevice blocks the route to the
south.
name: Rocky Downs

desc:
The land is steeply sloped here, descending from a line of hills on the west
into a deep, sheltered valley to the east. Two species of grass vie with one
another for dominance, one short and thick, the other taller and wispy. A few
weeds and wildflowers manage to poke their way through the grass, and a handful
of hawthorn trees rise over the land.
name: Grassy Slope

desc:
In the heart of the stretched-out grass-laden field, a solitary willow stands,
its trailing branches creating a sanctuary for the creatures of the field.To the
east, a grassy knoll gently rises, its modest elevation not competing with the
surrounding hills. To the west, the hills are crowned with brushes. On the north
and south, the steep slopes form a natural barrier.
name: Grass-laden Fields

desc:
These hills, garbed in green, are adorned with short, squat trees. A vivid green
carpet of moss clings fiercely to the hillside. Scattered across the hilltops,
large boulders stand, remnants of an age long past. To the east, a thin line of
hilltops rises. Towards the north, brush-covered hilltops peer over the
landscape. However, venturing west or south is a dream held at bay by the
formidable steepness of the hills.
name: Narrow Line of Hills

desc:
The hilltops gaze out over a landscape etched in stone and draped in green. Tall
firs and dark oaks tower over the hilltops, forming a stark contrast to the
undulating terrain. The terrain beneath the trees is a blend of stone and soil.
Step-like rock formations climb up to a rugged cliff shelf, their forms tracing
the contours of the hills. The hills continue to the south and west.
name: Thin Line of Hilltops

desc:
The surfaces of these hillocks are windswept, shaped, and caressed by the
constant zephyrs. Small clusters of wild marjoram are clinging to the crevices
in these hillocks. To the east, a rocky line of hills stretches towards the
horizon. To the north, a thin line of hilltops peeks over the horizon. The steep
hills to the west and south form an impassable barrier.
name: Windswept Hillocks

desc:
Shooting straight through a brush-covered plain, this more or less overgrown
roadway never seems to pause for breath. Muddy tracks made by coaches and
merchant carriages make it evident that the road is still being used, but
brambles and briars still threaten to trip the unwary traveller.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway runs north and south here, straight as an arrow. On the eastern
side of the road, a five-foot deep dike collects debris and serves as a runoff
for the rain. To the west, a line of thick brush creates a sort of wall of
undergrowth.
name: Greenway

desc:
Once a broad and busy highway, this road has now fallen into a state of
disrepair. Thick, thorny shrubberies lay to the west, while a deep dike bars the
way to the east. Farther east, the sloping ridges of the South Downs continue,
giving an undulating outline to the otherwise flat horizon.
name: Greenway

desc:
Once a great highway, this road has gradually grown into disrepair. Its surface
is covered by moss and grass except for the twin tracks made by the coaches and
wagons that still brave the journey. A wide trench, several feet deep, serves to
keep water from washing away what is left of the road.
name: Greenway

desc:
The Greenway makes a sharp turn here as it winds around the hills to the east
and the forest to the southwest. The road is hard, packed, dirt, with deep
grooves carved in two lines by heavy wheels of wagons. The corner of the bend
has begun to deteriorate into a muddy mess of a hole thanks to some careless
drivers' attempts to shave a few inches off their journey.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The road bends here, skirting the line of hills to the east. It is lined with
shallow ditches that allow water to run off the packed dirt into a deep, narrow
dike. An old fence sits just past the eastern edge of the road, slightly in
disrepair but still whole. A short trail juts off the Greenway through an
opening in the fence.
name: Bend in the Greenway

desc:
The road narrows here, as the hills rise to the east and the dike to the west
widens. Small pebbles and rocks line the dirt road, apparently the last vestige
of some attempts to hold back the mud. Deep tracks in two parallel lines
evidence the failure of that effort. Scores of extremely thick, gnarly bushes,
effectively obstruct all travel to the west and east of the road.
name: Greenway

desc:
The trail here is meandering and narrow. Dotting the landscape, enormous
boulders from the hills lie scattered about, cracked and weather-beaten. The
ground is strewn with sharp chips of rock. Amongst the rocks, bright flowers of
every hue burst forth. To the east, the trail winds and curls, leading to a
large wooden gate carved into steep hills which is the only way forward. To the
west, the trail beckons the traveller to the Greenway.
name: Trail to a Farm

desc:
The ridge is very narrow here as it begins to turn. Steep slopes all around
indicate that staying to the middle of the ridge is the safest option. Just down
the slopes to the west, the long line of the Greenway is visible for miles to
the north and south.
name: Narrow Passage in the Hills

desc:
The ridge of rocky hills makes a sharp turn here as it passes through the
plains. Below to the west, the long line of the Greenway continues its plodding
course north and south. The hills here are quite steep, limiting passage to the
north, while a gentle slope to the east leads down into a large basin.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The sun-baked meadows seem to stretch on forever, their flat, featureless
expanse broken only by the occasional rocky outcropping. Small tufts of brown
grass dot the landscape, their wispy tendrils waving in the hot breeze. The
meadows here are not uniform, for interspersed across the vast carpet of green
are noble firs, standing tall and solitary.
name: Sun-baked Meadows

desc:
Along the rocky hilltops, hidden crevices and caves are tucked away in the black
rock. This rocky wasteland has hardly any soil, and the scant earth that is
present supports minimal plant life. Its flat surface stretches out in all
directions, broken only by the occasional boulder or pile of rocks.
name: Rocky Hilltops

desc:
The hills drop off sharply to the west and south, giving an expansive view of
the wide valley between the Downs. The ridge continues to the north and east,
giving an elevated path through the terrain. A few rocks dot the hills, giving
it a pockmarked look among the otherwise green landscape.
name: Steep Hillside

desc:
In the very heart of this verdant sea, a lofty, stony apex rises. It stands as a
bastion amidst the rolling hillocks, its form a stark contrast to the soft
curves of the land. It is a sentinel, watching over the hills and valleys, its
presence a constant in this ever-changing landscape. The slopes of the apex have
recesses carved into their sides which bear the scars of time. These hollows are
cradles of life, nurturing pockets of boreal vegetation.
name: Apex in the Rolling Hills

desc:
The hills are marked by a few solitary trees, creating an open landscape amid
the undulations. The sparse trees pierce the skyline above the low-lying flora.
Occasional black spruce and balsam fir can be seen standing tall, yet with ample
space around them. Under their intermittent shadow, patches of feathermoss and
twinflower are scattered about.
name: Sparse Trees in the Hillocks

desc:
A ridge of hills, its form softened by time and weather, winds its way through
the South Downs, a natural pathway carved into the land. It is a living bridge,
tree-covered and vibrant, with paper birch and trembling aspen standing as
silent sentinels. Their roots delve deep into the ridge, anchoring them to the
land.
name: Ridge above the Hills

desc:
The South Downs unfold like a tapestry of rolling hillocks, a landscape draped
in hues of green and brown. A firm line of tall fir trees crowns the rocky
expanse, their needle-clad forms standing as silent sentinels over the land.
Dark oaks, ancient and proud, accompany them, their gnarled branches reaching
skyward in a silent proclamation of resilience. Among the hillocks, vegetation
thrives, the flora a testament to the land's nurturing touch.
name: Rolling Hillocks

desc:
The sides of the hills fall away steeply. Clumps of bearberry and crowberry
cling to the rocky slope, their forms huddled against the stone. In the heart of
the oak cluster, a lone, large boulder stands. Its surface is worn and
weathered, yet it stands unyielding.
name: Slope Down the Hills

desc:
The land here is covered in a verdant carpet of lush green grass, stretching as
far as the eye can see to the west and south, and up to the base of the hills to
the north and east. A small stream babbles along the bottom of the hill, its
gurgling provides soothing background noise. Resilient lichen that forms a
verdant carpet is cropped close to the earth. Indicating unseen grazers who find
sustenance here.
name: Green at the Base of a Hill

desc:
This meadow, resting in a deep valley, is cloaked in a light veil of mist that
rises off the slightly wet ground. The field spreads out, dotted with clusters
of wildflowers and tall grass, which hold onto the moisture with a gentle grip.
The atmosphere within the meadow is calm and soothing, with the quiet rustling
of the moist grass underfoot and the distant call of valley birds.
name: Misty Meadow

desc:
The valley lies nestled amidst the hills, shrouded in a mystical mist that
swirls and eddies around the bases of the trees, their trunks emerging from the
ethereal cloudiness. The terrain beneath is a unique kind of soil, marsh-like
and springy. It bounces back at each step, as if in playful jest.
name: Marshy Valley in the Hills

desc:
A low valley, several miles wide, passes through the hills of the South Downs
like a river flowing through a canyon. Covered in tall, green, grass, these
fields wave in the wind like the sail of some massive ship. A few flowers dot
the landscape, providing a bit of colour to the green expanse.
name: Wide Valley Passing through the Hills

desc:
Verdant waves of grass cover the landscape here in every shade imaginable. A few
flaxen flowers are the only break in the otherwise viridescent vegetation. The
grass is long, easily knee-high, but still smooth and silky like a finely woven
cloak.
name: Luscious Fields of Green

desc:
This patch of grassy fields marks an odd end to the otherwise open plains
between the hills. A deep dike east of the Greenway creates an impassable
obstacle to the west, while the hills to the north and east are so steep as to
make them equally unnavigable. Only the fields back to the south offer any way
out.
name: Patch of Grass Nestled in the Hills

desc:
This hilltop marks the northern end of one of the tall ridges of the South
Downs. A few lines of packed dirt, barely visible, mark where travellers have
left the safety and predictability of the Greenway to venture into the valley
between the hills.
name: Hilly Passage

desc:
The hills border this green valley to the west and south, while the valley
expands to the north and east. A few shrubs grow at the base of the hill, like
stitches at a seam. To the southeast, the valley widens even more like a canyon
through the plains.
name: Green Valley among the Hills

desc:
The wildflower meadow is a riot of colour, with petals of every hue imaginable
swaying in the breeze. The ground is covered in a soft carpet of green grass,
punctuated by small patches of wildflowers. Tall stalks of lavender jostle for
space with bright pink coneflowers, while delicate white daisies nod their heads
in the wind.
name: Wildflower Meadow

desc:
Amid this secluded valley, a clear, bubbling brook winds its way. Its melody, a
harmonious symphony of gentle ripples, echoes off the rocky walls. It nourishes
the land, bringing forth patches of bluebell and gorse, their colours a stark
contrast against the green and grey. Clustered around this brook are several
moss-laden boulders, their surfaces veined with lichen, creating an abstract
tapestry of life on stone. They serve as resting spots for the weary, a haven
for the small creatures of the valley.
name: Brook inside a Valley

desc:
At the edge of a great valley, a generous expanse of life nestles within the
embrace of the South Downs. Unapproachable they stand, their steep sides a
formidable wall to the north and east, a challenge to any who would venture
here. The lichen that thrives here has been cropped close to the ground by the
unceasing diligence of the valley's true artisans, the sheep. At the valley's
edge, a grove of hazelnut trees thrives. On the other side, a rocky outcrop
thrusts upwards, its surfaces adorned with lichen and moss.
name: Edge of a Wide Valley

desc:
The sunflower field stretches as far as the eye can see, an ocean of yellow that
bathes the landscape in a warm glow. The sunflowers themselves are tall and
proud, their petals soft and velvety to the touch, and their bright yellow heads
always facing the sun. Beneath the canopy of sunflowers, the soft rustle of
sunflower stems swaying in the gentle breeze creates a unique melody.
name: Sunflower Field

desc:
The rocky boulders, formidable and ancient, stand like a bulwark at this
secluded valley's boundaries. The valley, a verdant cradle, is adorned with a
carpet of soft, lush grass. The slopes, meanwhile, are sharp and unyielding, an
almost unscalable fortress of earthen and stone defences that nearly encircles
the valley. Narrow paths that slice through the monolithic boulders.
name: Middle of a Wide Valley

desc:
This yawning stretch of lowland is embraced by the gentle arms of the hills. The
hillsides are a tapestry woven of entangled shrubbery and sharp-edged boulders.
Straggling thorn bushes grow in profusion, their branches tangled in a wild
dance, their roots clutching the hardy soil, wrestling with the rocky terrain.
Scattered among the shrubs, sharp-edged boulders loom large.
name: Wide Valley between the Hills

desc:
A serpentine narrow passage cleaves through the ancient eminence of the South
Downs. The verdant hills, lush and solemn, rise on either side, their slopes
thickly dressed in a quilt of moss and lichen. Thorny bushes, bristling with
robust, sharp spikes, adorn the path, their toughened branches twisted into
gnarled labyrinths. Leaves of deepest green, veined with a lattice of white,
cluster densely and provide a sparse yet verdant canopy over the trail.
name: Narrow Pass into the Hills

desc:
The ground beneath is an undulating sea, the earth rising and falling in a
twisting rhythm. The hills morph from stony foothills into small hills westward.
Hardy mosses and lichens cling to the stone, their forms creating a verdant
carpet. Travel in these South Downs is fatiguing, the constantly shifting
landscape proving a challenge even for the most seasoned wanderer.
name: Twisting Line of Hills

desc:
The ridge snakes through the verdant landscape of the South Downs. Gazing
eastward, a steep overlook commands the view, a precipice of rugged majesty that
dares the bravest hearts to approach its edge. To the north, the terrain is
interrupted by a twisting line of foothills. Moss clings tenaciously to the
rocks scattered about, imbuing them with an almost ethereal, soft verdancy.
name: Narrow Winding Ridge

desc:
The overlook is an impressive precipice, an auditorium without a roof, with its
audience being the splintered rocks and its actors being the whispering winds.
In the crevices of boulders here, small, stiff bushes have found a home, their
roots gripping tenaciously. To the west, a narrow ridge winds, serpent-like,
across the landscape. It is a precarious pathway, where a single misstep might
lead to a heart-stopping plunge into the depths. North leads to a vast expanse
of grasslands.
name: Steep Overlook

desc:
The precipice of converging hilltops is scattered with fragments of scree,
shards of a once-solid edifice, now shattered and strewn about the hilltop
junction. Amidst these fragments, strange fungi make their homes, their caps
mottled in hues of sun-kissed yellow. To the south, the heart of the South Downs
undulates in rhythmic harmony. To the north, a steep overlook provides an
unobstructed view over the sprawling hills.
name: Junction of the Hilltops

desc:
The grasslands form a sea of green that sways in the wind like waves on the
ocean, framed by hills to the west, south, and farther north. The grass is tall
and lush, and wildflowers grow in clusters, creating a kaleidoscope of colours.
The only sounds are the rustling of the grass and the distant cries of birds.
name: Grasslands between the Hills

desc:
Blanketing the valley floor is a sprawling field, vibrant and alive. It's a
wide, tranquil expanse that sits nestled within the bosom of the valley. The
vale extends to the south and west, opening into rolling expanse of lush
meadows. Sparse trees adorn the surrounding hills to the north and east. The
ground underfoot is soft and wet, squishing slightly with each step.
name: Verdant Vale

desc:
Situated between steep hills on the west, south, and northeast, a lush valley
unfolds. The field, rich with soft, wet soil, stretches out like an inviting
carpet. On the slopes, solitary trees maintain a stately vigil. The serenity of
the glen is further accentuated by the occasional ripple of a breeze causing
soft waves across its verdant carpet.
name: Green Glen

desc:
The pasture is a riot of colour, with wildflowers of every hue covering the
ground like a multicoloured carpet. A gentle breeze blows through the area,
causing the flowers to sway gently in the wind. The sweet fragrance of the
flowers permeates the air, attracting bees and butterflies in droves.
name: Flowering Pasture

desc:
The meadow is shrouded in a fine mist that hangs low to the ground, lending the
surroundings an ethereal quality. A wide variety of flowers grow here, with
their petals dripping with dew. Butterflies, their wings shimmering in the soft
light, flit from bloom to bloom, sipping nectar as they go.
name: Misty Meadow

desc:
Despite the rugged terrain and sharp outcroppings of stone, the grasses here are
tenacious, their roots clinging to the rocky soil. The grasses are a deep shade
of green, with blades as sharp as razors, able to cut through flesh with ease.
As the wind whips across the grassland, the grasses bend and sway like the waves
of a tumultuous sea.
name: Rocky Grassland

desc:
A narrow passage snakes its way through the South Downs that rise in a spectacle
of earth and stone. This path, squeezed between the hills, looks like a thin
lifeline cleaved into the landscape. The terrain rises abruptly, with steep
hills full of rocks. The path is a thin trail, made of hard ground and scattered
stones, that turns and twists without warning.
name: Narrow Passage through the Hills

desc:
On these hilltops, paper birch and trembling aspen stand in sporadic clusters,
their bark gleaming silver and white, their leaves a vibrant green. Beneath
their dappled shade, a meadow of lowbush blueberries and bunchberries springs
forth, their round forms huddled close to the earth.
name: Undulating Hilltops

desc:
This is a small patch of grass, bordered by the Greenway to the north and east
and a small forest to the west and south. The waves of green blades are long,
broken up by the occasional wildflower or scrub bush. The grass is so thick in
some spots that it is difficult to see what lies beneath.
name: Wavy Fields of Green

desc:
This is a dark natural cavern inside the hill. Remains of people who must once
have been trapped are lying all around. Dank and unhealthy smells rise from the
ground, and rotting roots and vines line the dark, earthen walls. The only way
out of here is up through a stone lid that serves as a door.
name: In a Musty Hole

desc:
A large stand of trees has sprouted here, nestled between the downs to the west
and the Greenway to the east. Among the trees, a small pond has formed, covered
in a blanket of moss and green lily pads. To the south, the forest thickens with
broad, aged trees.
name: Small Pond in the Trees

desc:
Heavy and thick bushes form barrier-like obstacles here, making travel extremely
difficult. To the east, the hills of the South Downs rise over the plains. To
the south, the bushes and shrubbery turn to a circle of tall trees, providing a
sharp peak to the otherwise flat skyline.
name: Thick Bushes along the Greenway

desc:
Both visibility and travel are severely impaired by the dense shrubbery that
grows here. Among the wild bushes, black, moss-covered boulders further the
