inaccessibility of the area. To the north and south, the shrub-infested lands
continue, while to the east the Greenway is just visible through the growth.
name: Dense Bushes along the Greenway

desc:
A level plain is blanketed with dense thickets of thorny bushes. These plants
vary in height, ranging from waist-high to towering over a mounted rider,
creating a formidable challenge for navigation. The vegetation to the west is
impenetrable, rendering travel in that direction impossible.
name: Bushes along the Greenway

desc:
The thick, thorny, and generally inflexible nature of the bushes around here
makes it impossible to navigate the area properly. The bushes form an utterly
impenetrable wall to the west, while travel in other directions is possible by
cutting through gaps in the thorns. The straight line of the Greenway is just
visible through the shrubbery to the east.
name: Bushes along the Greenway

desc:
This is the site of an old, ruined farmstead. A long time ago, a house, a barn
and an outhouse stood here, but now the remains are almost completely hidden by
overgrowth, thick bushes, and briars. Brush so thick that not even the sun would
shine through it blocks all passage westwards, while other directions can
probably be negotiated with some care.
name: Bushes along the Greenway

desc:
Scores of thick, gnarly bushes effectively obstruct all travel in every
direction except to the south. The ground beneath the thorny brambles is almost
completely covered in a web of roots, with the bulkiest specimens thick as the
arm of a full-grown man.
name: Bushes along the Greenway

desc:
Thickets of thorns and brambles tangle themselves into a living labyrinth in
this underground cavern. The walls and floor are covered in a chaotic tapestry
of rotting leaves and various mosses. The decay adds a sickly sweetness to the
air, punctuated by the acrid tang of fungi. The leaves underfoot are damp and
spongy, muffling any sound of passage. The underground choked underbrush extends
to the south.
name: Choked Underbrush

desc:
In the tunnel, the constant rustling sounds echo from deep within the wall
foliage, their sources concealed by the enveloping darkness. Luminous patches of
bioluminescent fungus speckle the ground, casting an eerie, ghostly glow upon
their surroundings. Small sinkholes, long since abandoned by the burrowing
creatures who created them, lurk beneath the surface, waiting to swallow the
foot of the unwary. There are openings in the bushes towards the north and the
south.
name: Underground Tunnel

desc:
The walls and ceiling of the tunnel are composed of pitch-black soil. Puddles of
stagnant water lie pooled upon the muddy floor which provides sustenance to the
malicious poison ivy that creeps and twines its way through the underbrush, its
sinister tendrils reaching out. As the cavern continues northward, the way
becomes choked with loose rocks and forest debris, remnants of the ancient
forest that once stood here.
name: Deep Muddy Cavern

desc:
Sharp rocks to the north give way to sloping hills gently lowering to the south.
A deep gully obscures movement to the east. Small trees find their homes
scattered among the long mounds. Thorny brush huddles around each tree making
homes for small, snuffling creatures.
name: Secluded Hills

desc:
A faint cart path travels over a grassy hill, fading away almost entirely to the
north while becoming more prominent as it leads downhill towards a cluster of
buildings to the south. Standing to the east is a broken windmill, easily
accessible through a wide gap in its western wall. Another trail branches off
downhill to the west, showing signs of recent use by horse and man.
name: Overgrown Cart Path

desc:
Grasses and weeds grown between shallow ruts in the earth, clearly defining a
once often used cart path. It travels up a hill to the north, and to the south
terminates where it enters the large double doors of a sturdy barn. To the east,
a weary shed stands upon a rise. To the west, a wooded hill hinders travel in
that direction.
name: Overgrown Cart Path

desc:
A thin but well-worn trail traverses a narrow valley between the steep wooded
hills. The trail shows signs of being recently travelled by shod horses. To the
west is a large stable at the base of a hill. To the east, a windmill stands
upon a grassy rise, its blades looking skeletal and unmoving.
name: Trail through an Old Farmstead

desc:
A footpath passes through a neglected field, a fork of the path leading to a
large canvas tent to the south. The tent stands tall and sturdy, its stuctural
poles lashed tightly to nearby trees. A wooded hill east of you hinders travel
in that direction, but to the west and north the footpath continues its journey
through this valley between the hills.
name: Before a Large Tent

desc:
A thin trail travels over a rise in the grassy earth, granting a view of the
surrounding area. East of here can be seen a long canvas structure nestled
amongst hills and trees. To the west, the trail looks to curve through fields
towards a wooden structure southwest of here. Standing directly to the south is
a wooden hovel, leaning from age and misuse. Atop it has been built a crude
tower of lashed timbers. To the north there is a stable, but there is no
entrance to it from this direction.
name: Middle of an Old Farmstead

desc:
Low bushy weeds share the ground with longer grasses. The area is dominated by
green plants, but a few cracks can be seen where the dirt is visible. The grassy
knolls are all similar in height, too high to see over from the ravines between.
name: Weed Covered Hills

desc:
The hills here are long and wide without jagged features. Long grasses cover the
entirety of the ground. The grass is a lush green colour like well-watered moss
with occasional notes of darker greens. More hills can be seen in every
direction.
name: Lush Hills

desc:
This broad hill gently slopes down to the east with more severe slopes in other
directions. A few solitary trees give each other companionship on the otherwise
sparse land. Sparse grass and weeds are more visible at lower elevations. A
slight height advantage compared to the surrounding hills allows for
intermittent glimpses of the Greenway to the east.
name: Solitary Hill

desc:
Grassy hills cover the area, increasing in height towards the west until they
appear impassable. The taller hills are the only bits of land void of the lush
meadow that covers the rest of the area. Small animals could easily hide among
the waving blades of grass.
name: Imposing Hills

desc:
The hills are dominated by thick, green grasses of several varieties. Taller
grasses shoot up towards the sky before bending over lazily towards the earth,
unable to hold their full weight erect. A few narrow animal trails can be seen
winding paths through the grass.
name: Green Hills

desc:
A multitude of greenery and shrubs populate the area. Different species of
plants blend together into a wild undergrowth. Small rocks are mixed in with the
dry bushes. The weeds become more scattered towards the south where undulating
hills dominate the scenery.
name: Patch of Brush

desc:
This shed sits on a grassy rise at the base of a larger hill. Its western wall
is completely open, giving the building's occupants a clear view of a cart path
travelling through a valley. The walls and ceiling are built from roughly cut
boards, many of which are warped and broken. Rusted tools and empty burlap sacks
hang from the walls, signs that bags of grain and flour were once stored here.
name: Dilapidated Storage Shed

desc:
This windmill was once used to grind grain. A thin layer of grey dust covers the
interior and creates a haze in the air. Circular stone wheels lie broken away
from the wooden shaft that once drove them. The shaft travels from floor to
ceiling in the centre of the room.
name: Broken Windmill

desc:
The upper part of the windwill is more intact than the lower. Shafts and gears
come together to drive power from the wind to the grinding stones below. They
have not turned in quite some time. Amongst the mechanisms are at least a dozen
nests. A dirt-encrusted window with a broken pane creates a portal for birds to
come and go as they please. Many of their treasures (acorns, twigs and the like)
are strewn about the floor.
name: Cap of the Windmill

desc:
This old but sturdy barn served many functions. Small pens are built against the
western wall, in which chickens and goats once lived. The south wall holds
wicker drying racks from the straw covered dirt floor upward through a gap in
the plank ceiling. Between the racks a wooden ladder is attached to the wall,
leading to a hatch above. To the east, an open wall leads to another part of the
barn, while heavy doors in the northern wall lead outside.
name: Sturdy Barn

desc:
A lean-to is built off of the barn's main structure. It is wide enough to hold
two wagons side by side. Heavy metal rings are attached to the sides of the open
wall to the west, places to tie a horse as it waits to be hitched up. Dry pony
droppings are mixed into the dirt floor, giving the air a dusty, herbal scent.
name: Wagon House

desc:
Wicker drying racks adorn the walls of the loft, many of them still carrying
aged sheaves of wheat. Towards the centre of the floor, planking can be moved
aside to open a hole through which to drop sheaves of grain stalks into a wagon
below. A hatch in the floor near the south wall provides a safe way to climb
down.
name: Loft in a Barn

desc:
A wide alley travels between four horse stalls made from roughly cut lumber.
Simply crafted gates are latched closed. Halters and bridles hang on hooks
outside of each stall, appearing to be clean and recently used. A wide double
door leads outside to the east, while the stable itself extends further west.
name: Well Kept Stable

desc:
Wide wicker shelves have been built into the north and south walls of this room,
upon which are stacked loosely bound bundles of hay and bags of grain. The dirt
floor is swept clean of any debris. A wide doorway to the east gives the owner
of this stable easy access to feed any horses in the stalls. A small and sturdy
wooden door is set into the west wall.
name: Feed and Storage Room

desc:
This workshop is filled with horseshoeing equipment such as hammers, nails, and
a small anvil, as well as other tools for use in planting and harvesting grasses
and grains. The dirt floor is slightly damp, and the air has a musty odour. A
dirty window in the west wall looks out to a steep hillside. A door is set into
the east wall, while to the south a path through the clutter leads to a separate
part of the room.
name: Cluttered Workshop

desc:
The ceiling is lower here than to the north. Burlap sacks cover windows and hang
from the ceiling, placed as if to keep this room as dark as possible. A short
chain has been staked into the centre of the earthen floor, with leather
bindings tied to several of its rings.
name: Windowless Room behind a Workshop

desc:
A thin path turns through long abandoned fields, travelling east and south.
Scattered around not far from the path are occasional patches of disturbed earth
that could easily be unmarked graves, some more fresh than others. Hills tower
above to the north and west, providing no passage in those directions.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
Nestled at the end of this valley with hills rising above it to the west, south
and east is a farmhouse built of logs cut from local trees, standing upon a
foundation of mortar and stone. The only entrance appears to be through an open
porch to the west. A stone well sits roughly a hundred feet from the house.
name: Before a Farmhouse

desc:
A narrow porch with a wooden floor offers a sheltered entrance to this house.
The porch is bare, and every sound seems to echo off of its walls. The
floorboards creak under any movement across them, as if complaining constantly
about unwanted visitors. A simple door in the south wall offers entry to the
house proper.
name: Enclosed Front Porch

desc:
A sizeable gap six feet wide and ten feet long exists here between the wood
plank floor of the porch above you and the earth beneath. It is tall enough for
a man to almost stand stooped over. The foundation of the house and porch create
stone and mortar walls all around. The air is stale and musty.
name: Beneath a Porch

desc:
Even when it was new, this simple log home was not much to behold. Since then,
soft earth has slipped away beneath its western wall, causing it to lean
noticeably. Its once sloped roof has been mostly ripped away, replaced with a
crude tower of timbers lashed together with rope. As most of the chinking has
worn away from the cabin walls, there are ample footholds to reach the tower
above.
name: Leaning Hovel

desc:
Someone has turned the abandoned cabin below into a watchtower by replacing its
roof with a wooden rampart roughly built from lashed timbers. It is covered by a
simple roof made of poles and animal hides. From here its occupants can watch a
cart path that enters this farmstead near a windmill at least half of a mile to
the northeast.
name: Makeshift Tower

desc:
This canvas structure looks to be made of multiple peaked tents sewn together to
create a long hall of sorts. The peak of this section is open to allow smoke
from a fire pit in the centre of the room to escape. The northern wall of the
tent opens to the hills outside, while the southern wall opens to another room
deeper in the structure.
name: Long Canvas Tent

desc:
This peaked canvas tent creates a large room that is part of a longer structure.
In the middle, looking quite out of place, is an oaken table and eight chairs,
filled with dinnerware as well as an array of papers containing maps and lists.
The northern wall is open, leading towards the front of the long canvas hall. A
tent flap in the southern wall separates the rest of the structure.
name: Dining Area within a Tent

desc:
Cots made of wooden poles and canvas and covered by blankets are arranged tidily
throughout this tent. This room easily provides space for a dozen men to sleep.
A tent flap in a northern wall leads towards the front of the long canvas
structure.
name: Sleeping Quarters

desc:
The fissure in the earth leading outside to the south opens to a small cave
within the hill. Three natural pillars of stone and earth rise between the
uneven floor and ceiling. Scattered about are bones and debris, evidence that
this cave was inhabited by man or creature at one time.
name: Wide Fissure

desc:
Walls of dried, papery wood pulp and mud surround a large central chamber. They
are covered with millions of hexagon shaped cells where eggs are lain. It is
difficult to find solid footing on the sloped, spongy floor. There is a loud
buzzing coming from all directions. In one corner, the pulp and mud seems to
form a faint outline of a body.
name: Within a Massive Hornet Nest

desc:
A narrow tunnel, only a few feet wide in parts, travels on a zig-zagging course
through the earth east of here. The walls are a mix of rocks and compact dirt.
Roots from bushes and trees above criss cross the tunnel throughout, making it
challenging to traverse.
name: Narrow Tunnel

desc:
Sharp, bare rock covers all sides of this cylindrical tunnel. A few scratches
mark the walls, probably caused by some largel animal sharpening its claws. The
tunnel continues to the west and east, and a small draft of musty air passes
that way.
name: Rock Tunnel

desc:
Rubble and rock cover the floor of this narrow tunnel. A small path has been
cleared around the tunnel bend, apparently by the pacing of some large creature.
The ceiling is low, making each step a potential hazard for the tallest beings.
name: Rock-filled Tunnel

desc:
This simple farmhouse looks like it was once inhabited by a small family working
the surrounding land, but it has in recent times been ransacked. Furniture and
belongings have been shoved aside except for a wooden table dominating the main
room. One corner of the room is a sleeping area. It looks to be in order and
frequently used by the house's current occupants.
name: Ransacked Farmhouse

desc:
A simple cellar has been dug out beneath the farmhouse, its once earthen walls
reinforced by mortar and stone. Simple shelves adorn its walls, some of them
covered with aged carrots, turnips, acorns, and the like. The air holds a faint
odour of rot. The dirt floor is riddled with claw marks.
name: Cellar beneath a Farmhouse
